title: Changes in equilibrium
description: This exercise practices finding a new equilibrium price and quantity when supply changes, demand changes, or both supply and demand are changing simultaneously.`
topic_path: Market equilibrium and changes in equilibrium

assessment_items.question_content:
In the market for scooters, the price of one of the resources used to produce scooters has decreased. 

**Which of the following graphs show best represents how this market is affected by a decrease in the cost of producing scooters?**


[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: An increase in resource prices will decrease Supply.
When supply decreases, it shifts to the left.
When supply decreases, price increases and quantity decreases.

choices.content: ![A graph of a standard supply and demand model. The horizontal axis represents the quantity of goods (labeled as Q), while the vertical axis indicates the price levels (labeled as P). Two distinct supply curves are shown: S 1 and S 2 are lines that slope upwards from left to right, from the vertical axis. S 1 is positioned above S 2. A demand curve labeled D 1 is represented by a line that slopes downward from left to right. Two points, P 1 and P 2, are marked on the vertical axis. Two points, Q 1 and Q 2, are marked on the horizontal axis. Dashed horizontals from P 1 and P 2 join S 1 and S 2, and the same from Q 1 and Q 2, joins S 1 and S 2. assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is the best explanation of why the price adjusts following an increase in supply?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: A surplus will lead to prices decreasing. A shortage will lead to prices increasing.

choices.content: At the initial price, there will be a surplus after supply increases. Price must fall until the quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal again.
At the initial price, there will be a shortage after supply increases. Price must rise until the quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal again.
At the initial price, there will be a surplus after supply increases. Price must rise until the quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal again.
At the initial price, there will be a shortage after supply increases. Price must fall until the quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal again.
Price adjusts automatically when supply changes

assessment_items.question_content:
There has been a decrease in the supply of cheese in the market for cheese, as shown here. The initial supply curve is labeled $S_1$ and the new supply curve is labeled $S_2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What will be the new equilibrium as a result of the decrease in supply?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph of a standard supply and demand model. The horizontal axis represents the quantity of goods (labeled as Q), while the vertical axis indicates the price levels (labeled as P). Two distinct supply curves are shown: S 1 and S 2 are lines that slope upwards from left to right, from the vertical axis. S 2 is positioned above S 1. A demand curve labeled D 1 is represented by a line that slopes downward from left to right. Two points, P 1 and P 2, are marked on the vertical axis. Two points, Q 1 and Q 2, are marked on the horizontal axis. Dashed horizontals from P 1 and P 2 join S 1 and S 2, and the same from Q 1 and Q 2, joins S 1 and S 2. The intersection points E 1 and E 2 are the points where D 1 intersects S 1 and S 2.
options.caption: The market for cheese

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the sequences of events that lead to a price increase and quantity decrease when supply decreases?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: When supply decreases, there is a shortage because $Q_S<Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a surplus because $Q_S<Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a shortage because $Q_S>Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a surplus because $Q_S>Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the reason why price will increase when demand increases?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: At the old equilibrium price, the quantity demanded will exceed the quantity supplied, which will cause a shortage.
At the old equilibrium price, the quantity supplied will exceed the quantity demanded, which will cause a surplus.
Until price changes in response to a demand increase, the quantity demanded will be less than quantity supplied.
At the old equilibrium quantity, the price people are willing to pay for that quantity has decreased.
Demand will increase in response to the increase in supply which drives down the price of the good.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph here shows the effect of a decrease in demand on the market for cheese.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements about the decrease in demand shown in the graph is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph of a standard supply and demand model. The horizontal axis represents the quantity of goods (labeled as Q), while the vertical axis indicates the price levels (labeled as P). A supply curve S 1 is shown. The line slopes upwards from left to right, from the vertical axis. Two demand curves labeled D 1 and D 2 are represented by lines that slope downward from left to right. Two points, P 1 and P 2, are marked on the vertical axis. Two points, Q 1 and Q 2, are marked on the horizontal axis. Dashed horizontals from P 1 and P 2 join S 1, and the same from Q 1 and Q 2, joins S 1. The intersection points E 1 and E 2 are the points where D 1 and D 2 intersect S 1.
options.caption: The market for cheese

choices.content: The new equilibrium is $E_1$
The new equilibrium is $E_2$
The new equilibrium is $P=P_X$ and $Q=Q_A$
The new equilibrium is $P=P_Y$ and $Q=Q_B$
The new equilibrium is $P=P_Y$ and $Q=Q_A$

assessment_items.question_content:
Assume that milk is an inferior good.

***Assuming all other factors remain constant*, if the income of milk buyers increases, what will happen to the equilibrium price and quantity of milk?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When incomes increase for an inferior good, demand decreases.
When only demand changes, price and quantity will always move in the same direction.

choices.content: Price will decrease and quantity will decrease
Price will decrease and quantity will increase
Price will increase, but we cannot determine what happens to quantity
We cannot determine what happens to price, but quantity will increase
Price will increase and quantity will decrease

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for Good $A$ is currently in equilibrium as shown in this graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the demand for Good $A$ increases, which of the following is the most likely to be the new price and quantity in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph displays a supply and demand model. The vertical axis is labeled "P," which represents price, while the horizontal axis is labeled "Q," indicating quantity. Two lines are present in the graph. The downward-sloping line represents the demand curve, denoted as "D." In contrast, the upward-sloping line represents the supply curve, labeled "S." Three points, P 1, P 2, and P 3, are marked on the vertical axis. Three points, Q 1, Q 2, and Q 3, are marked on the horizontal axis. Dashed horizontals from P 1, P 2, and P 3 join S, and the verticals from Q 1, Q 2, and Q 3, join the same points.
options.caption: Good $A$

assessment_items.question_content:
Shampoo and conditioner are complementary goods.

**What happens to the price and quantity of conditioner if the price of shampoo decreases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price increases; quantity increases.
Price increases; quantity decreases.
Price decreases; quantity decreases.
Price decreases; quantity increases.
Price decreases; quantity demanded does not change.

assessment_items.question_content:
Brown sugar is an ingredient used to make churro waffles.

**What happens to the price and quantity of churro waffles if the price of brown sugar decreases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price decreases; quantity increases.
Price decreases; quantity decreases.
Price increases; quantity decreases.
Price increases; quantity increases.
Price increases; quantity does not change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which of the following scenarios would we definitely know that price will increase but we'd be unable to determine how quantity changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $D\uparrow$ and $S\downarrow$
$D\uparrow$ and $S\uparrow$
$D\downarrow$ and $S\downarrow$
$D\downarrow$ and $S\uparrow$
$D\uparrow$ and $S$ no change

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for music lessons has experienced a change in supply and a change in demand as shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What will definitely happen to the price and quantity of music lessons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

There are four lines plotted on the graph. The supply lines, labeled S 1 and S 2 slope upwards from left to right from points on the vertical axis. Line S 2 is above S 1. The demand lines, labeled D 1 and D 2 slope downwards from left to right from points on the vertical axis. Line D 2 is above D 1.

choices.content:
$\begin{align} &P \text{ increased}\\\\
&Q \text{ unknown}\end{align}$

$\begin{align} &P \text{ decreased}\\\\
&Q \text{ unknown}\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What happens to the price and quantity of cheese sold if the demand for cheese increases and the supply of cheese decreases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price increases; we do not know what happens to quantity.
Price increases; quantity stays the same.
Price decreases; we do not know what happens to quantity.
Price decreases; quantity increases.
Price decreases; quantity doesn’t change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What happens to the price and quantity of dog treats if the demand for dog treats increases and the supply of dog treats increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: We do not know what happens to price; quantity increases.
Price increases; quantity decreases.
Price decreases; quantity decreases.
Price decreases; quantity increases.
Price decreases; quantity demanded doesn’t change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Under what conditions will total economic surplus change the *most* in a market as a result of a change in demand?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When supply is very elastic and demand is very elastic
When supply is very inelastic and demand is very inelastic
When supply is very elastic and demand is very inelastic.
When supply is very inelastic and demand is very elastic.
When supply is perfectly inelastic and demand is elastic

assessment_items.question_content:
A change has occurred in the market shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff73bbee31ae1b383f519965fe1d6807aeb7cb73)

**What happens to consumer surplus and producer surplus when demand changes as shown in this graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Before demand increases, consumer surplus is $\$450$ and producer surplus is $\$900$.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$100 - \$70) \times 30\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$30) \times 30\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$900\\\\
&=\$450\\\\
\text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$70 - \$10) \times 30\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$60) \times 30\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$1800\\\\
&=\$900\end{align}$
After demand increases, consumer surplus is $\$800$ and producer surplus is $\$2400$.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$130 - \$90) \times 40\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$40) \times 40\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$1600\\\\
&=\$800\\\\
 \text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$90 - \$10) \times 40\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$80) \times 40\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$3200\\\\
&=\$1600\end{align}$

Both producer and consumer surplus increased.

choices.content: Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus increases.
Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus doesn’t change.
Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus increases
Consumer surplus decreases, producer surplus doesn’t change
Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus decreases

assessment_items.question_content:
A change has occurred in the market shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6e9525dda34c8320eab4d08059c4f5671d806334)

**What happens to consumer surplus and producer surplus when supply changes as shown in this graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Before supply decreases, consumer surplus is \$49 and producer surplus is \$24.50.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$23 - \$9) \times 7\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$14) \times 7\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$98\\\\
&=\$49\\\\\
 \text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$9 - \$2) \times 7\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$7) \times 7\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$49\\\\
&=\$24.50\end{align}$
After supply decreases, consumer surplus is \$36 and producer surplus is \$18.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$23 - \$11) \times 6\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$12) \times 6\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$72\\\\
&=\$36\\\\
\text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$11 - \$5) \times 6\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$6) \times 6\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$36\\\\
&=\$18\end{align}$
Both producer and consumer surplus decreased.

choices.content: Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus decreases.
Consumer surplus doesn’t change; producer surplus decreases.
Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus increases
Consumer surplus doesn't change, producer surplus increases

assessment_items.question_content:
A change has occurred in the market shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e512e6665d48ed0660fb77c4ebe0160d4f6f4b04)

**What happens to consumer surplus and producer surplus when demand changes as shown in this graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Before demand increases, consumer surplus is \$484 and producer surplus is \$242.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$70 - \$26) \times 22\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$44) \times 22\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$968\\\\
&=\$484\\\\
 \text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$26 - \$4) \times 22\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$22) \times 2\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$484\\\\
&=\$242\end{align}$
After demand increases, consumer surplus is \$144 and producer surplus is \$72.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$40 - \$16) \times 12\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$24) \times 12\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$288\\\\
&=\$144\\\\
 \text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$16 - \$4) \times 12\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$12) \times 12\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$144\\\\
&=\$72\end{align}$
Both producer and consumer surplus decreased.

choices.content: Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus decreases.
Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus doesn’t change.
Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus decreases
Consumer surplus doesn't change, producer surplus doesn’t change
Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus increases

assessment_items.question_content:
A change as occurred in the market shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/295a19591cdbb761e672be7f34bf35582bcc78ef)

**What happens to consumer and producer surplus as a result of the change shown in this graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Before supply increases, consumer surplus is \$9 and producer surplus is \$4.50.


$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$20 - \$14) \times 3\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$6) \times 3\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$18\\\\
&=\$9\\\\
 \text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$14 - \$11) \times 3\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$3) \times 3\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$9\\\\
&=\$4.50\end{align}$
After supply increases, consumer surplus is \$25 and producer surplus is \$12.50.

$\begin{align} \text{Consumer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$20 - \$10) \times 5\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$10) \times 5\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$50\\\\
&=\$25\\\\
\text{Producer surplus} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\$10 - \$5) \times 5\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} (\$5) \times 5\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \$25\\\\
&=\$12.50\end{align}$
Both producer and consumer surplus increased.

choices.content: Consumer surplus increases; producer surplus increases.
Consumer surplus doesn’t change; producer surplus increases.
Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus decreases
Consumer surplus decreases; producer surplus increases
